speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Hello everyone. Welcome to a new episode of The Dissenter. I'm your host, as always, Ricardo Lops, and today I'm joined for a 4th time by Dr. Christian Miller. He's the AC Reed Professor of Philosophy at Wake Forest University, and today we're going to talk about his latest book, The Honest. The Crisis preserving our most treasured virtue in an increasingly dishonest world. There is the cover of it. So Christian, welcome back to the show. It's always a great pleasure to

speaker: Christian Miller
words: everyone. It's great to see you again. It's a delight to talk to you. I'm thrilled to be back and talk about honesty.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Yeah, sure. So look, we've already done a conversation on this topic before, but let's talk a little bit more about it. And for people who perhaps haven't watched our first interview on honesty, tell us first what is honesty and how do you approach it from a Philosophical perspective because I would imagine that we could also approach it from a psychological perspective and maybe we're going to talk about some of that later when we talk about the science of dishonesty of honesty and dishonesty, but from a philosophical perspective, how do you approach it?

speaker: Christian Miller
words: Yeah, sure. So, and this is I should say for listeners who haven't heard the earlier interview, I'm not gonna presuppose any background. And actually, I wrote this book, uh, from scratch without assuming anyone has read anything by me before. So, uh, I'm thinking of honesty from a philosophical perspective as a character trait, uh, As one of the virtues, as part of our character. And so it's not just a matter of an honest action, like one time I tell the truth, or one time I don't cheat on a test. It's more than that. It's a matter of behavior and a matter of internal psychology, but it has to be consistent over time and consistent across situations. So let me unpack that a little bit more. Um, SO an honest person is going to act. Honestly, in a variety of circumstances, whether that's the podcast interview, whether that's the party, whether that's the, in the classroom, at, at home, at the office, stably over time, so not just on Tuesdays, but every Tuesday, every day of the week, every month of the year, um, in an honest manner. When honest questions or issues arise. Now, that might sound pretty straightforward, because you might think honesty is just a matter of telling the truth, and that's what we normally gravitate towards, uh, you know, Pinocchio, Abraham Lincoln, all of our kind of cultural icons are associated with telling the truth. But actually, I think it's a lot more nuanced and A lot more sophisticated than that. Um, HONESTY has to do with telling the truth for sure, but it also has to do with preventing cheating, preventing stealing, preventing BSing or bullshitting, hypocrisy, self-deception, fraud, uh, misleading statements. So, it covers a lot of moral territory on the behavioral side. It governs a lot of our behavior. Plus, and just to wrap it up here, um, it has to come from the right kind of heart. So outwardly, you could behave honestly, and people could say, oh, that must be a really honest person, but unless we know what the motivation is for that behavior, why it's being done. We can't really say for sure whether that person is honest or not. And so they could be doing these actions purely for self-serving reasons. They could be um trying to advance in the company or not get punished in the company by getting fired. They could want rewards in the afterlife, they could want to avoid feeling guilty, make a good impression on others, get social media followers, all these kinds of reasons. Uh, COULD motivate honest behavior, but if they're just about me and benefiting myself, they don't count as virtuously honest. It has to be something, uh, that's outward looking. Altruistic, or at least not egoistic, not self-centered, if we're talking about the virtue of honesty. And so, I can unpack that more uh on the motivational side, but to sum it up, it's actually a multifaceted, nuanced, kind of complex virtue with behavioral components, as well as internal psychological components.

It's not an original example, it's a very famous one, it's the Nazi at the door example. Um, IT actually has an earlier iteration in Immanuel Kant's philosophy. Kant had an example involving an ax murderer who was looking for a victim, and you're hiding the victim, would-be victim. And then you lie to the ax murderer to save the person's life. Um, THE updated version of this is the Nazi at the door, so you're, it's World War II in a German-occupied village. You're a homeowner, you're protecting a Jewish family. So you're faced with this dilemma that, you know, at least this challenge, what do I do? Well, you might think it's obvious. I think it's pretty clear what to do. I think you lied to the Nazi. Um, NOT everyone has always agreed with that, so Augustine, um, Aquinas probably, and Kant himself, so three huge names in the history of philosophy, all said that lying is always wrong. And so it seemed like they would, uh, take the opposite view, but overwhelmingly today when I asked my students, when I ask other people, uh, what would you do, and not just what would you do, but what should you do, um, they say it's morally permissible, indeed, morally obligatory, so that you're morally required to lie to the Nazis, such that if you don't lie to the Nazi, you're doing something morally wrong. Um, SO that has the seemingly paradoxical consequence that sometimes dishonesty can be morally obligatory. But I don't think that's too paradoxical because I think when we're talking about moral obligatory, all things considered. With respect to honesty, yes, wrong, but all things considered, not just honesty, but also compassion for the Jewish family, trying to protect innocent lives, all that kind of thing, when we weigh in all the considerations, then it's morally obligatory to lie to the Nazi at the door.

The Kantian case is by far the hardest, um, although I think even there it's, it's, uh, it's interestingly, um, not so straightforward, so. Take Kant's first formulation. The picture is of a mixed bag here with some good sides to our character, some bad sides to our character. So most people are not good enough to count as honest. Most people are not bad enough to count as dishonest. It's a mixed character. Uh, LET me, let me, uh, highlight a little bit of the good and a little bit of the bad. More recent research though, has found that, well, if you actually look at individuals, And survey them, most people don't lie, or at least don't report that they lie. We can, of course, question that self-report of lying. Um, SO in one study in particular, uh, 60% of people said they've not told a single lie in the last 24 hours. Most of the rest of the people told very few lies in the last 24 hours. Only 5% of people told a significant number of lies in the last 24 hours. So, this has led to an actual well established theory called truth default theory, which says that most people tell the truth most of the time. That we default into truth telling. That's our default mindset. We're not neutral. Like, I might tell the truth, I might lie. I have to kind of decide, and we don't default into lying. We default into telling the truth, and we default into assuming others are telling the truth to us. So we're default truth biased in what we expect from other people. So I think overall we can, of course, challenge that and question it in some ways, but, but I think that's, that's pretty positive, um. Now, on the flip side, um, let's switch to a different kind of behavior, let's switch to cheating. When you put people into different situations where there's a monetary incentive to cheat.
